quote: I just got a Tesla. And as I was thinking about it, I was like, hey. What if I just charge this off of the sun? And then, you know, we got a huge roof line. You'll see it. We've got, you know, a van. I mean, it's, like, six or 7,000 square feet.
stewart_read: Kyle delivers the codex's designated emotional anchor — interest_in_solar — with a specific, personal, high-value motivation: a new Tesla, home charging intent, and a 6,000-7,000 sq ft home. The rep's response ('That's exactly what we'll be able to show you') acknowledges the answer but does not expand it. Per verify.interest_in_solar, 'depth matters more than the answer' and 'top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' The rep spent approximately 10 seconds before pivoting to Google Maps. This is the primary verify failure on the call — not an understandable miss given the customer's openness, but the most costly one.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that Kyle's motivation was already clear and that dwelling on it would feel redundant to a customer who had already articulated it well.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this moment and ask: 'What would you have asked next?' The codex says this is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch. Kyle gave a gift here — EV charging, large home, curiosity framing. A 60-second expansion ('Tell me more about the Tesla — how far are you driving it? What does your charging look like now?') would have built the bill-flip foundation the rep never laid. This is the root cause of the flat energy shape.

quote: 4,000 kilowatt hours is there after the year. I think we're paying, like, 15¢ kilowatt hours, so 500 something bucks, 600.
stewart_read: Kyle self-calculates a $500-600/month bill and hands it to the rep. The rep responds 'Okay. Gotcha. Sounds good.' and moves to confirm the kWh average. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the required pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — the rep executed 'ask + acknowledge + move on,' which the codex identifies as the universal floor failure. The flip opportunity here was unusually high-leverage: Kyle is already paying $500-600/month to Duke AND is about to add EV charging load, meaning his bill is structurally going up. The rep had the numbers to make that point and didn't.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been holding the bill-flip for the closer's proposal conversation, treating the setter call as pure data collection.
coaching_implication: This is the bill_anchoring coaching beat in its clearest form. The rep had the number, the customer, and the EV context to execute a flip ('That $600 is about to go up — you just added a Tesla to that bill'). No fabricated numbers needed; Kyle provided them. Walk the rep through the flip mechanic using this exact moment.

quote: Colder and hotter months are a little bit higher. Yeah. Milder months are a little bit lower. Gotcha. Okay. Sounds good. But 4 k is a good middle ground.
stewart_read: Kyle elaborates on seasonal variation — a second, unprompted invitation to anchor bill pain. The rep's 'K. Perfect.' and immediate pivot to the credit qualifier treats this as a data-confirmation moment rather than an emotional one. This is the third bill-data touch (after 00:52 and 02:16) and the third time the rep collected without converting. The seasonal detail is particularly useful because it signals that Kyle's bill peaks in summer and winter — the exact months when solar's value proposition is highest.
coaching_implication: Pair this moment with the 02:16 entry in the 1-on-1. The pattern is the same both times: Kyle offers more than the rep asked for, and the rep treats it as a checkbox confirmation. The coaching question is: 'When a customer gives you more than you asked for, what does that tell you?' The answer is that they're engaged and want to talk about it.

quote: Yeah. My wife. It's actually in her name, I think, officially.
stewart_read: Kyle discloses that the home is in his wife's name — she is the title holder, not a co-owner. This is a harder version of the spouse-on-title scenario: the primary decision-maker is not on the call. The rep's response is 'Oh, okay. To look here. Yeah. Yeah. No problem at all.' and moves to utilities. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct response is to include the spouse in the appointment — 'What time works for both of you to be on the call together?' The rep did not execute this protocol. The appointment is booked with the non-title-holder, which is a structural vulnerability for the sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that Kyle is sufficiently engaged and motivated that the wife's presence is a closer-stage concern, not a setter-stage one.
coaching_implication: This is a protocol gap, not a persuasion gap. The codex is explicit: 'If spouse on title, they MUST be on the appointment.' The rep's 'No problem at all' is the wrong move — it accepts a structurally fragile booking. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'If the wife is the title holder and she's not on the call, what happens when the closer tries to close?' Then walk through the protocol template.

quote: Awesome. Well yeah. And then I'll make a note here, that you got a Tesla. So are you planning on charging from home then?
stewart_read: The rep circles back to the Tesla detail and confirms home charging intent — this is the right instinct. But the follow-through stops at 'Let me make note of that, and we can, yeah, just kinda go over what that looks like as well.' The Tesla Powerwall is the one Ion product that directly answers Kyle's stated motivation: charging his Tesla from solar. Ion carries the Powerwall as its only battery product. The rep had the setup — EV, home charging, large roof, high bill — and did not name the Powerwall as the product that closes the loop on Kyle's own logic. This is not a closer-tier conversation; naming the product as a reason to see the specialist is setter-appropriate.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have intentionally left product specifics for the closer, treating the Powerwall mention as scope creep.
coaching_implication: The distinction here is between selling the Powerwall (closer's job) and naming it as the reason the appointment is worth Kyle's time (setter's job). A suggested rephrase: 'We actually carry the Tesla Powerwall battery — that's the piece that lets you store what your panels generate and charge your car from it. That's exactly what our specialist will walk you through.' This is curiosity creation, not product selling. Walk the rep through that line.

quote: No. Actually, I've got something tomorrow. Okay. But What what what let's look like two weeks out. So I got a few things, and I got something coming up next week. So let's looks like two weeks out.
stewart_read: Kyle pushes the appointment two weeks out due to a busy schedule. The rep accepts without any attempt to find an earlier slot or anchor urgency. A two-week gap on a warm, self-motivated lead is a no-show risk — the customer's enthusiasm is real today and may cool by the seventh. The rep's own button-up acknowledges this implicitly ('just because it is kinda scheduled out a little bit'). The conditional nature of this booking — two weeks out, wife not confirmed as participant, no verbal handshake — compounds the fragility.
alternative_hypothesis: Kyle was clear about his schedule constraints; pushing harder for an earlier slot risks friction on an otherwise smooth call.
coaching_implication: The rep correctly accepted Kyle's constraint rather than arguing it. The gap is in what came next: no urgency anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no wife-inclusion ask. The coaching beat is not 'push harder for an earlier date' — it's 'when the date is two weeks out, the button-up protocol becomes more important, not less.' Walk the rep through the full button_up checklist and ask which elements were completed on this call.

quote: Last thing to do now is we can just find a good time for one of our specialists to stop by, just kinda go over the options here with you, play around with the design there
stewart_read: 'Stop by' implies an in-person visit. The codex specifies button_up.phone_appointment_specified as a required element, and the industry briefing defines the product as a 30-minute phone appointment. The rep's language here sets a wrong expectation that the closer will physically visit Kyle's home. This is not an ambiguous case — the codex is unambiguous on phone appointment specification as a required button-up element. If Kyle is expecting an in-person visit and receives a phone call, the no-show risk increases.
coaching_implication: Pull this line in the 1-on-1 and ask the rep: 'What is the specialist doing on Friday the seventh?' If the rep says 'calling Kyle,' ask them to listen back to what they told Kyle. The fix is a single sentence: 'One of our specialists will give you a call — it's a 30-minute phone appointment.' Also check whether the rep's button-up included the no-driving and at-computer anchors — neither appears in the transcript.

quote: you know, at this point, I suggest just writing down any questions or concerns you can just possibly think of just so we don't miss any information you're looking for.
stewart_read: This is a positive signal in an otherwise thin button-up. The rep's 'write down your questions' close is a genuine curiosity-creation move — it frames the appointment as Kyle's opportunity to get answers to his own questions, not a sales pitch he has to sit through. Per coaching_philosophy.curiosity_over_features, the setter's job is to make customers curious to see their specific math. This line does that. It partially offsets the missing verbal handshake and phone-appointment specification by leaving Kyle in an active, question-generating posture toward the appointment.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this move explicitly in the 1-on-1 — it's the one button-up element that landed well. Then use it as a bridge: 'You did this part right. Now let's talk about what else the button-up needs.' The contrast between this line and the missing 'is that fair' handshake is a useful teaching frame.
